intent:
The UI-thread half of the slow-disk complaint. `slow_disk_big_library`
shows the render thread idle-clean while the sweep grinds, but sitting
still is not what the user does — they fly the grid. This drives the
gesture path (pan, then a real pinch stream, then the release) over a
REAL library of thousands of tiles while the background sweep is loose
on the drive and live lanes are up.

`pinch_ribbon` covers the same gesture against `--demo` tiles: no
decoders, no workers, no disk. That isolates layout cost, which is the
right control. THIS scenario is the contended version — same gesture,
but everything else in the app is also running. If the two disagree, the
gesture is not what costs; the contention is.

What to read:
  - Render-thread stalls: any non-zero count over 50/100ms is a visible
    hitch, and over 250ms is the "app hung" report. `pinch_ribbon`'s
    prose already names the shape to fear — a lone multi-hundred-ms max
    on the release frame, which is an O(library²) walk.
  - Phase attribution during the gesture. The gesture phases (layout,
    ribbon walk) are deliberately NOT in the attributed set, so if the
    stall tail is real and `ingest_install`/`drain_media`/`live` are all
    small, the time is in layout — exactly where the invariant says a
    regression would land.
  - Served-frame gaps: whether the live lanes keep delivering while the
    main thread is busy laying out. These are separate threads, so a gap
    here during a gesture means contention, not blocking.

Tier A caveat, and it is a big one for this scenario: there is no GPU
and no present. Upload stalls and present-to-present gaps — a plausible
home for a gesture hitch on a real library — are Tier B only. A clean
run here does NOT clear the gesture; it narrows the suspect to the half
Tier A cannot see.
